s1.text: My brain is just replaying everything.

s3.text: I'm awake at eleven, or three, or both.

s4.text: Every email I sent. Everything I might have done wrong.

s5.text: What Anjali will say if I'm late again.

s6.text: What if I'm not good enough for placements.

s7.text: My chest gets tight. Like I can't pull a full breath.

s8.text: My hands go kind of numb sometimes.

s9.text: I dropped two of my tutoring kids last week.

s10.text: I left a paper half-done because I couldn't focus.

lines.text: She is already seated when you walk in.
Phone face-down on her lap. Hair tied back. A polite half-smile, and then she is looking at the window.
Somewhere in the ceiling a fluorescent light is humming in a key nobody can name.

choices.text: Sit down.

lines.text: Hi. Um.
Sorry, I don't really know how this works.
Is it okay if I just... talk?

choices.text: Of course. Take your time.
Tell me what brought you in today.
You sound nervous. That's okay.
Nod, and stay quiet.

lines.text: Okay. Um.
So my friend Anjali, she's the one who told me to come.
I didn't... I mean, I don't think anything's wrong exactly. I just haven't been sleeping.
Like, at all. Well, I sleep. But it's... not sleep.

choices.text: Not sleep. What does that mean for you?
When did this start?
What does Anjali notice that you might not?
Okay, we can probably fix this with better sleep hygiene.

lines.text: It's like my body is sleeping.
But my brain is just replaying everything.
Every conversation I had that day. Every email I sent. Everything I might have done wrong. It just loops.
It's been happening since April? Maybe before.
I'm awake at eleven, or three, or both.

choices.text: Let her keep going.
That sounds exhausting.
Every email. That's a lot of replay.

lines.text: I keep thinking, what if I sent it too casual. What if the reply comes tomorrow and it's bad.
Every email I sent. Everything I might have done wrong.
And then what Anjali will say if I'm late again.
And then my tutoring families. And then I'm back to the emails.

choices.text: Is there one worry that comes up more than the others?
When your mind loops like that, does it ever stop?
What happens in your body when this is happening?
Have you tried not thinking about it?

lines.text: It's affecting things.
I dropped two of my tutoring kids last week.
I couldn't face telling their parents in person. I did it over WhatsApp.
I'm behind on a project submission.
I left a paper half-done because I just... couldn't focus on it.
I don't know. I feel like I'm the kind of person who doesn't do this.

choices.text: What does 'the kind of person who does this' look like to you?
That must be hard when you're used to coping.
Dropping the tutoring sounds responsible, actually.
How are your parents taking it?

lines.text: There's this...
My chest gets tight. Like I can't pull a full breath.
Mostly when I'm checking my inbox.
My hands go kind of numb sometimes.
I thought it was like a heart thing, so I looked it up. The internet was not helpful.

choices.text: Let the moment breathe.
Looking it up at three AM is a special kind of hell.
Have you had anyone check your heart?

lines.text: I don't know why I'm like this.
It's not like I have big problems.
Some people have actual things going wrong. I just have... the usual things. Placements. My mum. The usual things.

choices.text: Tell me about placements.
Tell me about your mum.
You said 'some people have actual things.' What counts as actual?
You're probably just stressed about exams.

lines.text: Placement season started in April. Everyone's got a plan. I don't.
My CGPA is fine but that's not enough now.
And I keep thinking, what if I freeze in an interview.
What if I'm not good enough for placements.
And I end up sitting at home while everyone else is... you know.

choices.text: What would that mean to you? Sitting at home.
Have you had an interview yet?

lines.text: One. Last week.
I don't know how it went. I keep replaying it.
They asked me about teamwork and I said this thing about group projects and I don't know if it landed.
I've been checking my email since Thursday.

choices.text: Say nothing.
Checking your email for four days is its own kind of marathon.

lines.text: My mum is... she's fine. She's kind. She worries.
My dad doesn't say much.
He didn't speak to my brother for like a week when he dropped a year.
I think about that sometimes.

choices.text: What do you think about when you think about that?
Does your mum know you're here today?

lines.text: I haven't told her.
I just think... I'm the one who doesn't drop anything.
That's my whole thing.
I'm not allowed to.

choices.text: Stay with the silence.
Nobody tells you that out loud, do they.

lines.text: I don't know.
Actual things are like, cancer. Or someone died. Or you didn't have food growing up.
I had food. I have a BCom seat. I don't get to be sitting here.

choices.text: You're here though. What brought you past that thought?
Being here doesn't require permission from how bad it is.
Tell me about placements.

lines.text: Sorry. I haven't actually talked about any of this to anyone.
It feels weird to hear it out loud.

choices.text: It's supposed to feel weird. That means it's working.
Take your time.
Let me ask you a harder question.

lines.text: I... no. No, nothing like that.
I just want to sleep.
I get frustrated with myself for not being able to, like, get it together. But not... not that.

choices.text: Thanks for being honest with me about that.
Have you ever felt that way, even briefly?
Do you have people around you?

lines.text: I mean, I'm tired all the time, yeah. Food is whatever.
I'm not... sad, exactly?
Like I can still laugh at things with Anjali.
I went to a wedding two weeks ago and I actually had fun, for like three hours.
I think I'm just wound.

choices.text: Wound. That's a good word for it.
When you say tired, do you mean sleepy tired or can't-get-up tired?
What do you enjoy, when your brain isn't looping?

lines.text: So.
What is it.
Is something wrong with me.

choices.text: What you're describing is anxiety. The kind that runs constantly in the background. It's very common and it's treatable.
It sounds like you might be depressed. I'd like to talk about that.
I'd want to rule out some physical things before giving this a name.
Honestly, I think you're stressed about exams. It'll pass.

lines.text: Maybe. Yeah.
Um. Sorry, I think I'm wasting your time.
This was... a bad idea.
I'll figure it out.

choices.text: Please stay. I hear I just missed it. Can I try again?
I don't think this is a waste of time. Sit with me another minute.
Okay. Think about coming back.

lines.text: Anxiety. Okay.
That actually... that makes sense.
I think I'd been making up worse things in my head.
What do I do with it?

choices.text: Walk her through the plan.

lines.text: You talk her through it. A trial of a low dose SSRI if she is open to it. A cognitive therapy referral. Sleep hygiene that doesn't pretend this is just about sleep.
She nods through it. When you're done, she picks up the phone, and puts it in her bag.
"Same time next week?"
Maya agrees politely.
She stopped pushing back two questions ago.
She leaves with a prescription and a follow-up card.
She will take the medication. It may even help.
But she walks out still believing her worry is just who she is.
At 3 AM her brain will replay every email again.
"Yeah, okay. Thank you for your time."
She is already standing. The phone is in her hand.
At reception, she does not book another appointment.
Anjali will ask how it went. Maya will say it was fine.
She will not come back this year.
